name: Percy
description: Isomorphic web apps with a virtual DOM (html! macro) and SSR, an early entrant

description.value: A collection of Rust + WebAssembly libraries for building isomorphic (client- and server-rendered) frontend web apps with a virtual DOM and an html! macro
description.comment: Repo tagline 'Build frontend browser apps with Rust + WebAssembly. Supports server side rendering.'; the book frames Percy as a collection of libraries, not a single crate.

license.comment: Dual-licensed MIT OR Apache-2.0 per crates.io metadata for percy-dom.

crates-io.comment: percy-dom is the top-level virtual-DOM crate; the project also publishes html-macro and percy-router in the same workspace.

documentation.comment: The Percy Book; API docs also at https://chinedufn.github.io/percy/api/percy_dom/. The book states it is a work in progress with some placeholder chapters.

first-release.comment: Project's first crates.io publish was as virtual-dom-rs on 2018-07-25; the crate was later renamed/refocused to percy-dom (first published 2021-07-11).

latest-release.comment: crates.io publish date of percy-dom 0.10.2; checked 2026-07-19.

maintenance-status.comment: Latest commit 2026-07-07 and 0.10.2 released 2026-07-06 (both within 6 months); development is sporadic and essentially single-maintainer but ongoing.

maturity.comment: Still pre-1.0 (0.10.2) and the book intro states Percy 'is not yet ready for production (unless you're incredibly brave)'; long-lived but self-described as not production-ready.

corporate-foundation-backing.comment: No corporate/foundation sponsor; effectively driven by a single maintainer (chinedufn) with occasional contributors.

reactivity-model.comment: percy-dom is a standalone virtual-DOM with diff/patch; no fine-grained signals. App state updates are wired manually (Elm-like Store pattern in the isomorphic example) but the core render path is VDOM diffing.

component-model.comment: Components can be plain functions returning a VirtualNode or structs implementing the View trait (render).

view-authoring.comment: Views are written with the html! macro.

fine-grained-updates.comment: Uses virtual-DOM diff/patch rather than fine-grained signal-targeted updates.

uses-virtual-dom.comment: percy-dom is 'A standalone Virtual DOM creation, diffing and patching implementation'.

async-await-in-components.comment: No async component / resource-style .await data loading documented in the book.

global-state-solution.comment: No first-party global-state primitive; the isomorphic example implements its own app-state Store passed through the app crate.

compiles-to.comment: Client compiles to WebAssembly via wasm-bindgen; the same view code renders to a String on the native server side.

primary-build-tool.comment: Examples build the client crate with wasm-pack; no Trunk or first-party build tool.

official-cli.comment: The book intro says a CLI for generating a starter project is planned 'once the tools have stabilized' but none is published yet.

dev-server-hmr.comment: Examples use a wasm-pack build plus a hand-written server script; no first-party hot-reload or live-reload dev server.

minimal-wasm-bundle-size.comment: No reliably documented, methodically-measured hello-world gzipped .wasm size available; per RESEARCH.md, prefer null over an unmethodical number.

js-interop.comment: percy-dom builds on wasm-bindgen with web-sys/js-sys for browser/JS API access (these deps are optional for server-only virtual-node use).

js-interop-ergonomics.comment: Standard wasm-bindgen/web-sys path with the usual boilerplate; no dedicated JS-interop conveniences or documentation beyond the underlying crates.

no-std-minimal-runtime.comment: Recent work made virtual-node's wasm-bindgen/web-sys optional for non-browser (SSR) use, but there is no no_std mode.

client-side-rendering-spa.comment: Client crate compiles to WASM and renders/patches the DOM in the browser (baseline SPA mode).

server-side-rendering.comment: SSR out of the box: html! views render to a String on the server (dedicated 'Server Side Rendering' book chapter).

static-site-generation.comment: No first-party static-site-generation tooling; render-to-string could be scripted at build time but there is no dedicated SSG feature.

hydration.comment: No documented DOM-hydration mode; the isomorphic example serializes initial state to JSON so the client boots with the same state and renders client-side, rather than hydrating the server-rendered DOM.

server-functions.comment: No integrated isomorphic server-function mechanism; client/server communication is written manually.

streaming-ssr.comment: SSR renders a complete String; no streaming SSR documented.

backend-integration.comment: SSR produces a String usable with any server; the isomorphic example ships its own small server crate rather than integrating a specific framework.

isomorphic-routing.comment: percy-router is used both server-side (to render the matching view for SSR) and client-side in the isomorphic example.

official-router.comment: percy-router is maintained in-tree by the core team as part of the Percy workspace.

nested-routes.comment: Router docs cover type-safe URL params and an on-visit callback only; no nested/child route layouts documented.

dynamic-route-params.comment: Routes support parameterized segments parsed into typed handler arguments (type-safe URL params).

typed-routes.comment: Route path is a string literal in the #route(path = "...") macro; parameters are type-safe, but routes are not enum/derive-based, so an invalid path is not a compile error (per RESEARCH.md's strict typed-routes bar).

route-guards.comment: The router offers an on_visit callback (side effects on entering a route) but no blocking navigation guard / redirect hook.

lazy-code-split-routes.comment: No first-party lazy / code-split route loading documented.

styling-approach.comment: First-class CSS-in-Rust via the css! macro (write styles next to html! components); external/plain CSS also works. The book's CSS-in-Rust chapter is still a TODO placeholder.

scoped-styles.comment: The css! macro generates a unique class name per invocation, so CSS-in-Rust styles are effectively component-scoped and do not leak globally.

component-library-ecosystem.comment: Essentially no ready-made UI component-library crates; users build their own components.

forms-inputs.comment: Inputs are wired manually via events (e.g. oninput) reading/writing app state; no two-way-binding directive or dedicated form helpers.

svg-canvas-support.comment: The html! macro handles the SVG namespace (dedicated svg_namespace handling in html-validation), so SVG elements can be authored in views; canvas is reachable via web-sys.

suspense-loading-ui.comment: No declarative suspense / loading-boundary primitive documented.

documentation-quality.comment: The Percy Book covers the html! macro, router, and SSR, but is self-described as a work in progress with placeholder chapters (e.g. CSS-in-Rust is 'TODO'); API docs exist on the docs site but examples and tutorials are thin.

api-ergonomics.comment: The html! macro is a familiar JSX-like API, but the manual state/Store wiring and multi-crate (app/client/server) isomorphic layout add boilerplate.

compile-times.comment: Macro-based html! views; comparable to peer macro-driven Rust web frameworks for a small app.

examples-count.comment: About 4-5 example projects in the repo (component-preview, embed-non-percy-node, isomorphic, unit-testing-components, css-in-rust), i.e. the 3-10 'limited' band.

interactive-playground.comment: No online playground/REPL or hosted example gallery; the isomorphic example README notes a live demo host is still a TODO.

testing-support.comment: The book has a 'Unit Testing your Views' chapter and a unit-testing-components example (render views and assert); browser tests use wasm-bindgen-test.

crates-io-downloads.comment: All-time downloads of percy-dom; checked 2026-07-19. The predecessor crate virtual-dom-rs adds ~43k more.

contributors.comment: ~25 contributors per the GitHub contributors API; checked 2026-07-19. Development is dominated by the maintainer chinedufn.

community-size.comment: Effectively single-maintainer with ~25 historical contributors and ~2.3k stars; no dedicated chat/community hub advertised.

ecosystem-crates.comment: First-party router and html! macro live in-tree, but there are essentially no third-party component kits or integrations.

notable-production-users.comment: No widely-documented production users; the book itself calls Percy not yet production-ready.

meta-framework-full-stack-kit.comment: No separate meta-framework / full-stack kit; the isomorphic SSR story is assembled from the core Percy libraries (app/client/server crates) rather than a dedicated fullstack layer.
